disclaimer: Prototype content adapted from public WHO/UNICEF Infant and Young Child Feeding (IYCF) guidance. NOT yet reviewed by a qualified health professional. Must be clinically validated before any real-world use.

tips.text: For the first 6 months, feed your baby only breast milk — no water, porridge, or other foods. Breast milk alone is enough during this time.
Start breastfeeding within the first hour after birth. This helps protect the baby and supports your milk supply.
tips.source: WHO/UNICEF IYCF — early initiation of breastfeeding

tips.text: Breastfeed on demand, day and night, as often as the baby wants — this is normal and helps your milk supply increase.
At 6 months, start adding soft, mashed foods alongside continued breastfeeding. Do not stop breast milk — add food on top of it.
tips.source: WHO/UNICEF IYCF — introduction of complementary foods

tips.text: Try to include a mix of foods each day — grains, beans or groundnuts, vegetables, fruit, and if available, eggs, fish, or meat — for a well-rounded diet.
tips.source: WHO/UNICEF IYCF — minimum dietary diversity guidance

tips.text: Keep breastfeeding alongside other foods until your child is at least 2 years old.
Feed your child patiently, encourage them gently, and watch for hunger and fullness cues rather than forcing them to finish.
Continue feeding your child small, frequent meals during illness, and offer extra food after recovery to help them catch up.
tips.source: WHO/UNICEF IYCF — feeding during and after illness

tips.text: Eat a variety of foods during pregnancy and take iron-folic acid supplements as advised by your health worker.
tips.source: WHO IMPAC — maternal nutrition during pregnancy

tips.text: Continue eating a varied diet and drink enough fluids while breastfeeding — your body needs extra energy during this time.
